title: Lumea de Ieri, Lumea de Azi și Lumea de Mâine
summary: The article analyzes Ursula von der Leyen's controversial speech declaring Europe can no longer be the guardian of the old world order, drawing parallels with Stefan Zweig's 'The World of Yesterday' to argue that Europe must reinvent itself rather than cling to nostalgia or abandon its values. It critiques both von der Leyen's apparent willingness to discard values along with outdated forms, and her critics who defend abstract principles without proposing concrete solutions for a changed geopolitical reality.
talking_points: Von der Leyen declared Europe can no longer guard the old world order and that EU unanimity in foreign policy is an obstacle to geopolitical credibility
Critics like Antonio Costa and Teresa Ribera pushed back, defending multilateralism and international law as fundamental premises
The parallel with Stefan Zweig illustrates the danger of European intellectuals refusing to engage with messy political realities while clinging to abstract principles
The rules-based international order is already in clinical death: US-Israel bombing Iran without UN mandate, China ignoring international courts, Russia invading Ukraine with limited consequences
Europe must do what Schuman and Monnet did in 1950 — pour old values into completely new forms rather than choosing between abandoning values or clinging to obsolete structures
author: Andrei Colțea
